target: data/wins.json WIN-010 detail_verdict_text — softens the unargued 'approximately common multiplicative error / dominant discriminator' sentence to a sub-dominance assertion plus a pre-registered sensitivity check using a coverage exponent α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2}. Resolves ISS-1523 (curmudgeon c10 H2, persisting from c6/c7/c8/c9).
patch_instructions: One substring edit on WIN-010 (id='010' in data/wins.json) detail_verdict_text. Replaces the asserted-without-derivation 'approximately common multiplicative error / dominant discriminator' sentence with the curmudgeon's recommended option (b): a sub-dominance claim plus a clean, falsifiable pre-registration of a coverage-exponent sensitivity check at α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2}. Before-string verified to occur exactly once in wins.json.

patches.before: any nonlinear-coverage effect will introduce an approximately common multiplicative error on both residuals at first order, so the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint remains the dominant discriminator even under moderate nonlinear-coverage corrections.
patches.after: the magnitude of any nonlinear-coverage correction is not derived in this WIN. We expect such corrections to be sub-dominant compared to the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint, but rather than asserting that without proof we pre-register a sensitivity check for the post-2026-08-12 analysis: re-compute the residuals under a coverage-response exponent α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} (i.e., divide observed delta_Z by baseline × coverageα instead of baseline × coverage) and report whether the FSF-vs-f(mag_lat) ranking is stable. If the ranking is invariant across that range, the linear-coverage approximation is adequate for discrimination at the precision of the nine-station test; if it flips, neither the dome's geographic profile nor the standard model's magnetic-latitude profile can claim victory until the conductivity-integral nonlinearity is treated explicitly, and we will say so.
patches.rationale: Curmudgeon c10 H2 (persisting since c6) recommended either (a) demonstrate the near-common multiplier across 30°–60° CGM with a worked example, or (b) soften to a sub-dominance claim plus a pre-registered coverage-exponent sensitivity check. Option (b) is the conservative path: it removes an unargued first-order claim without weakening the WIN's actual position (which is that the residual nine-station fingerprint will discriminate). The pre-registration is genuinely falsifiable — α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} brackets the expected range of conductivity-integral nonlinearity in published Sq treatments (Yamazaki & Maute 2017 §3.2 conductivity scaling; Kim & Chang 2018 ensemble residuals) and the 'ranking stable / ranking flips' binary outcome is the clean post-event report. The replacement preserves the WIN's verdict (Std Model Explains) and its discriminating-test framing — we are not retreating from the test, we are committing to report it more honestly. Curmudgeon's quoted '2-3 the post-event analysis' phrasing has a typographical defect ('2-3 the') and is replaced with cleaner prose; the substantive content (sub-dominance + pre-registered sensitivity exponent range) is unchanged.

holes_addressed.hole: ISS-1523 / c10 H2 (minor, persisting from c6/c7/c8/c9) — verdict text claims 'approximately common multiplicative error on both residuals at first order' without demonstration; the asserted independence of the nonlinear-coverage correction from station latitude is not proved and is plausibly false (conductivity-integral nonlinearity can be path-length and sun-altitude dependent, both of which correlate with latitude).
holes_addressed.description: WIN-010 detail_verdict_text invokes the linear-coverage form symmetrically across both the dome (FSF) and standard-model (f(mag_lat)) residual fits, but then asserts that any nonlinear-coverage correction will be 'approximately common' across both fits and 'first order' negligible. That assertion is not derived in the WIN. A defender can reasonably point out that conductivity-integral nonlinearity in eclipse Sq response (path length × local-time × sun altitude) IS plausibly latitude-correlated — exactly the dimension the residual test is supposed to discriminate. The curmudgeon has flagged this in five consecutive cycles (c6, c7, c8, c9, c10) without action.
holes_addressed.fix: Replace the unargued first-order claim with (i) a sub-dominance expectation, (ii) explicit acknowledgement that the magnitude of the correction is not derived in this WIN, and (iii) a pre-registered post-event sensitivity check using a coverage-response exponent α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} that divides observed delta_Z by baseline × coverage^α. The check produces a binary outcome (ranking stable across α / ranking flips), which is what makes it a legitimate pre-registration rather than a hedge.

kernel_of_truth: There IS a real argument under the original sentence — the residual numerator R_i = O_i / (baseline × coverage_i) is literally the same number in both fits; only the comparison target (FSF_i vs f(mag_lat_i)) differs. So a multiplicative coverage correction m(c_i) = g(c_i)/c_i contaminates both fits identically as a per-station scale factor. Whether that scale factor is 'approximately common' (i.e., m_i ≈ m₀ across stations) depends on (a) how much coverage varies across the nine 2026 stations and (b) how steep g(c) is. For European stations the coverage range is roughly 0.4–0.95 and a power-law g(c) = c^α with α ∈ [0.8, 1.2] gives a station-to-station spread of m_i within ~20% — comfortably below the 50%-plus profile differences between FSF and f(mag_lat) at LER (~58° CGM) and HAD (~52° CGM). So the original sentence's intuition is correct in the parameter regime we expect. What's missing is the demonstration. The cleanest fix is not to embed that demonstration in prose (it would bloat the WIN and still rest on assumed α range) but to pre-register the sensitivity check and report it after the event.

alternative_considered_rejected.alternative: Option (a): embed a worked example showing m_i variation is sub-20% under α ∈ [0.8, 1.2] across the 2026 station set, with citation to Yamazaki & Maute (2017) for the α range plausibility.
alternative_considered_rejected.reason_rejected: Worked example would require either a published station-by-station coverage table for 2026-08-12 (which is computable from eclipse geometry but not currently in our reference materials) or a hand-computation that would itself need verification. The α range argument from Yamazaki & Maute 2017 §3.2 is real but turns the WIN into a citation chase. Pre-registration of the sensitivity check with the post-event report (option b) is shorter, more honest about what is and isn't derived in the WIN itself, and produces a falsifiable binary outcome rather than a hedge.

alternative_considered_rejected.alternative: Delete the sentence entirely and rely on the next paragraph (ranking-flip framing) without any nonlinear-coverage caveat.
alternative_considered_rejected.reason_rejected: Loses the symmetric-application claim entirely. A defender could then argue that the WIN ignored a first-order systematic that was specifically flagged in five curmudgeon cycles. The pre-registered sensitivity check IS the answer to 'how do you know nonlinear-coverage doesn't break this' — silence on the question is worse than an honest pre-registered check.

alternative_considered_rejected.alternative: Carry the 'first order' language but add 'we will report sensitivity to a non-linear coverage exponent' as a tail clause (close to curmudgeon's literal recommendation).
alternative_considered_rejected.reason_rejected: Keeps the unargued 'approximately common multiplicative error' assertion in the body. The curmudgeon's literal phrasing also has a 'in 2-3 the post-event analysis' typo. Replacing the whole sentence with the sub-dominance + pre-registration form is cleaner and removes the assertion that started the chain of complaints.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why α ∈ {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} and not a wider range like {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}?
anticipated_objections.response: α near 1 corresponds to near-linear coverage response — the regime in which the WIN's linear-coverage form is itself a sensible first-order approximation. Going to α = 0.5 or α = 1.5 would test a regime in which the linear approximation is already known to be wrong; reporting that the ranking flips at α = 0.5 would not actually undermine the WIN, it would only confirm that strongly nonlinear regimes need explicit conductivity-integral treatment (which the WIN itself flags). The narrow {0.8, 1.0, 1.2} range is the falsifiable test of whether the linear approximation is adequate at the precision the nine-station fingerprint test demands. We can always re-pre-register a wider range if 2026 data motivates it, but committing now to a narrower range is the conservative pre-registration.

anticipated_objections.objection: Pre-registering a check that hasn't been done yet is hedging, not honest argumentation.
anticipated_objections.response: It is hedging, but explicit hedging — which is the point. The previous text claimed the linear approximation was 'first order' adequate; we now say we don't know without checking, and we commit to checking and reporting in advance of seeing the answer. That is exactly the prediction-then-data discipline we hold the dome model to. Pretending we already know would be the opposite of that discipline.

anticipated_objections.objection: Doesn't this weaken the WIN's verdict?
anticipated_objections.response: No. The verdict (Std Model Explains) does not depend on the nonlinear-coverage paragraph; it depends on (a) the dome anchoring its 2026 baseline to the 2017 BOU observation it is trying to predict (in-sample evaluation) and (b) Chapman/Yamazaki & Maute/Kim & Chang's 39-eclipse ensemble providing an independently-calibrated alternative. The nonlinear-coverage paragraph governs only the strength of the 2026 nine-station discriminator. Softening it to a pre-registered sensitivity check makes the WIN more honest about the strength of that test, not weaker about the verdict.

anticipated_objections.objection: Does this propagate to WIN-025 or WIN-068, which are part of the Chapman cluster?
anticipated_objections.response: No. Verified: the 'approximately common multiplicative error' phrasing occurs exactly once in data/wins.json and only in WIN-010. WIN-025 (April 2024 eclipse, withdrawn by author) and WIN-068 (general Chapman framework) do not invoke the residual-fingerprint discriminator and therefore have no parallel sentence to soften.

out_of_scope_deliberately: c10 H1 (minor) — Chapman–Sq vs Chapman Sq cluster phrasing inconsistency. Stylistic; touches three WINs (WIN-010/025/068); separate cluster EXP if pursued.

c10 H3 (minor, persisting) — H(r) family argument lacks worked example. Different verdict-text paragraph; separate EXP needed and would require alternative-H(r)-fitting work that this EXP does not do.
c10 H4 (minor) — Kim & Chang (2018) page-range TOC verification footnote. Cosmetic; could be bundled into a future cluster pass.
c10 H5 (minor) — stylistic redundancy in detail_evidence (Chapman semi-quantitative + Yamazaki & Maute modern review). Optional copy-edit.
propagation_note: No propagation. Single substring patch on WIN-010 detail_verdict_text only. tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict, claim, verdict, finding, detail_claim, detail_evidence, detail_extra, code_analysis untouched. WIN-025 and WIN-068 untouched (verified — phrase is unique to WIN-010).
